questions.question: Explain the term 'software crisis' and mention two reasons that led to it.
keyPoints: Software crisis refers to the problems faced in software development in the late 1960s
Reasons: increasing complexity of software projects
Lack of standardized development processes
Poor documentation
Difficulty of maintenance
Unreliable software
Growing demand for functionality

questions.question: List and explain any four essential attributes of software.
keyPoints: Functionality: the degree to which software performs its required functions
Reliability: the ability to perform consistently over time
Efficiency: utilization of resources like memory and processing power
Usability: ease of learning and operating the software
Maintainability: ease of modification to fix defects or improve performance

questions.question: Differentiate between system software and application software with examples.
keyPoints: System software: manages computer hardware and provides platform for applications - examples: Windows OS, Linux, compilers
Application software: performs specific user tasks - examples: MS Word, Photoshop, web browsers
System software is close to hardware while application software is close to users
System software runs automatically while application software is user-initiated

questions.question: Why is software considered different from hardware despite being part of a computer system?
keyPoints: Software is intangible - has no physical form unlike hardware
Software does not wear out through use but becomes obsolete
Software is developed not manufactured physically
Software is flexible and can be easily modified
Hardware requires physical changes while software changes are logical
Software aging is different from hardware aging

questions.question: Define software engineering and explain its primary objectives.
keyPoints: Software engineering is the systematic approach to software development
It applies engineering principles to creation and maintenance
Objectives: produce high-quality software
Meet user requirements within budget
Deliver on time
Ensure software is reliable and maintainable
Address the software crisis

questions.question: What is meant by software maintainability? List two factors that affect maintainability.
keyPoints: Maintainability is the ease with which software can be modified
Factors: code complexity - simpler code is easier to maintain
Quality of documentation
Modular design with loose coupling
Use of standard programming practices
Availability of testing procedures

questions.question: Explain the concept of code reusability and its benefits in software development.
keyPoints: Code reuse is using existing software components to build new applications
Benefits: reduces development time and cost
Improves reliability through tested components
Increases consistency across applications
Easier maintenance since changes propagate
Promotes standardization and better design

questions.question: Discuss the evolution of software engineering as a discipline. What problems led to its emergence and how does modern software engineering address them?
keyPoints: Software crisis in 1960s-70s: projects over budget, delayed, unreliable
NATO conference 1968 formalized software engineering
Traditional engineering principles applied to software
Modern approaches: structured methodologies (Waterfall, Spiral)
Agile methodologies for flexibility
CASE tools for automation
Standardized processes (CMM, ISO)
Emphasis on testing, documentation, maintenance
Component-based development and reuse

questions.question: Explain the various categories of software based on application area. Give examples for each category and discuss their characteristics.
keyPoints: System software: OS, compilers, device drivers - manages hardware
Application software: business apps, scientific apps, productivity tools - user-specific tasks
Embedded software: in appliances, vehicles, devices - hardware-dependent
Web software: web apps, cloud services - network-based
Custom software: bespoke applications - specific client needs
Each category has different development, testing, and maintenance requirements

questions.question: What are the key differences between software and hardware? How do these differences affect software development practices?
keyPoints: Hardware is tangible, software is intangible
Hardware wears out, software becomes obsolete
Software is flexible, hardware is rigid
Software development is creative, hardware manufacturing is repetitive
Software cannot be 'manufactured' like hardware
These differences lead to: different testing approaches
Different maintenance strategies
Need for documentation
Importance of version control
Emphasis on flexibility and maintainability in design

questions.question: Discuss the essential attributes of quality software. Explain why each attribute is important for software success.
keyPoints: Functionality: must meet user requirements
Reliability: consistent performance without failures
Efficiency: optimal use of resources
Usability: easy to learn and operate
Maintainability: easy to modify and enhance
Portability: can run on different platforms
These attributes affect: user satisfaction
Development costs
Maintenance efforts
Software lifespan
Competitive advantage
